3A_Bibliographic.citation: Kiss, O., Alzueta, E., Yuksel, D., Pohl, K. M., de Zambotti, M., Muller-Oehring, E. M., Prouty, D., Durley, I., Pelham, W. E., McCabe, C. J., Gonzalez, M. R., Brown, S. A., Wade, N. E., Marshall, A. T., Sowell, E. R., Breslin, F. J., Lisdahl, K. M., Dick, A. S., Sheth, C. S., ... Baker, F. C. (2022). The Pandemic's Toll on Young Adolescents: Prevention and Intervention Targets to Preserve Their Mental Health. Journal of Adolescent Health, 70(3), 387-395.
3A_Bibliographic.notes: Author list inferred from DOI metadata; full author list not available in parsed text.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We included data from adolescents who completed the ABCD protocol (ABCD 3.0 data release) at prepandemic visits and online COVID-19 surveys (May-August 2020). ABCD data from the 3.0 data release included all participants who had completed the Year 2 assessment by February 2020.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: N varies by model: Survey 1 N=3,091; Survey 2 N=3,193; Survey 3 N=2,934. Using 3,193 as primary analytic N (Survey 2 which had stress, anxiety, positive affect models).

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Screen time during COVID-19 pandemic (multiple types: social media, video games, browsing internet, watching news, etc.) - one of many predictors in machine learning model
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: ABCD COVID-19 survey items on screen time activities during the pandemic
3C_Exposure.modalities: social media, single-player video games, browsing internet, watching coronavirus-related news, listening to music/watching music videos
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Various pandemic screen time items used as predictors in XGBoost classification model; not a primary focal exposure
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: screen time (e.g., time spent on social media)... Several screen time variables appeared in the top predictors of anxiety, with higher frequency of 'watching news related to coronavirus,' more time 'playing single-player video games,' or 'browsing the internet,' predicting anxiety.
3C_Exposure.notes: Screen time is NOT the primary exposure. This is a machine learning prediction study where screen time is one of hundreds of features. The study examines predictors of pandemic mental health broadly.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Psychological distress during COVID-19 pandemic: positive affect, perceived stress, anxiety, depressive symptoms
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: NIH Toolbox (Positive Affect, Depressive Symptoms, Anxiety scales); Perceived Stress Scale (PSS-4)
3D_Outcome.base_rate_if_binary: Cutoff at T-score > 60 for NIH Toolbox measures; raw score >10 for PSS. Depressive symptoms: 16.49% above cutoff in Survey 1, 14.04% in Survey 3.
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: National Institutes of Health (NIH) toolbox measures designed for those aged 8-17 years were used to assess emotional wellbeing (Survey 2: Positive affect) and negative affect (Survey 1 and 3: Depressive symptoms; Survey 2: Anxiety) during the past week. The 4-item Perceived Stress Scale (Survey 2: PSS) provides a brief measure of stress perceptions during the last month.
3D_Outcome.notes: Outcomes were binarized for classification models (T-score > 60 cutoff).

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: XGBoost (Extreme Gradient Boosting) classification models with SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values for feature importance; nested cross-validation; AUC for model evaluation
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: N/A - machine learning model with >200 features simultaneously including demographics, prepandemic measures, pandemic psychosocial and lifestyle factors
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: We trained regularized logistic regression (LogReg) and XGBoost models (GBT) separately with several possible parameters to find the best fitting model targeting positive affect, depressive symptoms, stress, and anxiety... For model evaluation, we used area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) metrics. For model comparison we used nested cross-validation... To interpret these nonlinear models, we relied on SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) values.
3E_Design_Analysis.notes: This is a machine learning prediction study, not traditional regression. No conventional effect sizes (betas, ORs) are reported. Results are SHAP values indicating feature importance.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: SHAP value not numerically reported for screen time in positive affect model; described as one of the top 20 features. Less screen time predicted higher positive affect.
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: healthy behaviors during the pandemic (more physical activities, less screen time), and younger age predicted high positive affect

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Watching news related to coronavirus - pandemic screen time
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: SHAP feature importance; among top 20 features for anxiety model. Higher frequency predicted more anxiety.
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Several screen time variables appeared in the top predictors of anxiety, with higher frequency of 'watching news related to coronavirus,' more time 'playing single-player video games,' or 'browsing the internet,' predicting anxiety.

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Playing single-player video games - pandemic screen time
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: SHAP feature importance; among top 20 features for anxiety model. More time predicted more anxiety.
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: more time 'playing single-player video games,' or 'browsing the internet,' predicting anxiety

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Browsing the internet - pandemic screen time
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: SHAP feature importance; among top 20 features for anxiety model.
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: more time 'playing single-player video games,' or 'browsing the internet,' predicting anxiety

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Listening to music/watching music videos - pandemic screen time
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: SHAP feature importance; among top 20 features for depression model at both timepoints.
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: 'listening to music' during the pandemic were in the top features associated with depressive symptoms at both timepoints

3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Machine learning study reporting SHAP values and AUC rather than traditional effect sizes. No conventional betas/ORs/CIs reported for screen time predictors. The study appropriately notes that prediction does not indicate causality.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: it is important that all adolescents receive regular and convenient access to mental health care or social/community-based support programs... modifiable lifestyle-related factors such as sleep may be the first approach to improving adolescents' psychological wellness.
3H_Recommendations.notes: Recommendations focus on mental health access, sleep, and healthy behaviors broadly - not specifically on screen time reduction.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: modifiable lifestyle-related factors such as sleep may be the first approach to improving adolescents' psychological wellness
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion focuses on identifying protective and risk factors for adolescent mental health during COVID-19. Screen time is discussed as one of many predictors, not the primary focus. The paper is cautious about causal interpretation, stating prediction does not indicate causality. It emphasizes social connectedness, sleep, and physical activity as modifiable targets more than screen time. The discussion is balanced and appropriately nuanced about the role of digital connections during pandemic isolation.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: No traditional effect sizes (betas, ORs) are reported for screen time associations. SHAP values are reported only in figures, not numerically for all screen time predictors. Screen time is a secondary finding in this paper, making it difficult to extract conventional effect sizes for the screen-time-to-outcome association.
